perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall safeguard the Confidential Information at least to the same extent that it would its own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall endeavor to safeguard the Confidential Information to the same extent that it deems appropriate for its own confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'endeavor' weakens the obligation to safeguard information. 'Deems appropriate' introduces subjectivity, undermining the requirement for consistent protection of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: DUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, and the modified text's subjective 'deems appropriate' may not meet that standard, particularly if the party's self-determined level of protection is insufficient.
perturbation.location: B. Confidential Information - General Non-Disclosure Provisions, 1. Disclosure of Confidential Information.

Trade Secrets Â§ 2001. Definitions. The following words, terms and phrases, when used in this chapter, shall have the meanings ascribed to them in this section, except where the context clearly indicates a different meaning: (1) âImproper meansâ shall include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) âMisappropriationâ shall mean: a. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or b. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade was: A. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; B. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or C. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of the personâs position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) âPersonâ shall mean a natural person, corporation, statutory trust, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) âTrade secretâ shall mean information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process, that: a. Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being rea
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 2002 Injunctive relief. :: 2015 Delaware Code :: US Codes and Statutes :: US Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Delaware Code 2024 Delaware Code 2023 Delaware Code 2022 Delaware Code 2015 Delaware Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2015 Delaware Code Title 6 - Commerce and Trade CHAPTER 20. TRADE SECRETS § 2002 Injunctive relief. 6 DE Code § 2002 (2015) What's This? (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. 63 Del. Laws, c. 218, § 1; 71 Del. perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'shall safeguard...to the same extent that it would its own' with 'shall endeavor to safeguard...to the same extent that it deems appropriate.' This introduces a subjective standard ('deems appropriate') and weakens the obligation to 'endeavor,' potentially falling short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to maintain secrecy, as explained in the Law Explanation. While the scraped snippets do not explicitly define 'reasonable efforts,' they establish the importance of maintaining secrecy to protect trade secrets under Delaware law, particularly § 2001, which defines 'improper means' as including 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' The modified text creates an ambiguity as to whether sufficient measures are being taken to safeguard confidential information. Thus, the change is significant enough to create a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: A Reviewing Representative shall not use the Confidential Information or any portion thereof to give any Party or a competitor of any Party a competitive or commercial advantage.
perturbation.changed_text: A Reviewing Representative should generally avoid using the Confidential Information in a manner that clearly provides a direct competitive or commercial advantage to any Party or their direct competitors.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not use' to 'should generally avoid' weakens the prohibition. Adding 'clearly provides a direct' and 'direct competitors' introduces subjectivity and allows for interpretation, potentially enabling misuse of information.
perturbation.law_explanation: DUTSA defines misappropriation broadly, and the modified text allows room for Reviewing Representatives to argue that they are not in violation of the contract while potentially still using the information in a way that is legally considered misappropriation.
perturbation.location: B. Confidential Information - General Non-Disclosure Provisions, 2. Reviewing Representative.

TRADE SECRETS § 2001 Definitions. 6 DE Code § 2001 (2015) What's This? The following words, terms and phrases, when used in this chapter, shall have the meanings ascribed to them in this section, except where the context clearly indicates a different meaning: (1) "Improper means'' shall include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation'' shall mean: a. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or b. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade was: A. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; B. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or C. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of the person's position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person'' shall mean a natural person, corporation, statutory trust, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret'' shall mean information, including a formula,
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall not use" to "should generally avoid using" weakens the original prohibition. The added qualifications of "clearly provides a direct" and "direct competitors" introduce subjectivity, allowing for potential misuse of confidential information. This contradicts the spirit of trade secret protection as defined in the provided snippets from Delaware Code Title 6, Chapter 20, particularly § 2001, which defines 'Misappropriation' as 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. By introducing ambiguity, the modified text creates a loophole where a Reviewing Representative could argue their actions don't meet the threshold of 'clearly providing a direct' advantage, thus potentially circumventing the law's intent to prevent unauthorized use of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party and Reviewing Representative shall defend against disclosure of Confidential Information pursuant to any Third Party Request through all available legal processes, including, but not limited to, seeking to obtain any necessary protective orders.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party and Reviewing Representative should consider defending against disclosure of Confidential Information pursuant to any Third Party Request, potentially through available legal processes such as seeking protective orders, as they deem reasonably advisable.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall defend' to 'should consider defending' makes the obligation discretionary. Adding 'as they deem reasonably advisable' introduces a subjective element, further weakening the defense against disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 26(c)
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 26(c) provides a framework for seeking protective orders to protect confidential information during discovery. The modified text makes seeking such protections optional and subject to a party's discretion, potentially undermining the ability to maintain confidentiality in legal proceedings, which goes against the spirit of protecting trade secrets.
perturbation.location: B. Confidential Information - General Non-Disclosure Provisions, 6. Defend Against Third Party Requests.

perturbation.justification: The original text states that each party 'shall defend' against disclosure of confidential information, which creates a mandatory obligation. The changed text weakens this to 'should consider defending' and adds 'as they deem reasonably advisable,' making the action discretionary. While the provided Delaware Courts snippet is a 'Page Not Found' error and provides no direct statutory language, the Law Explanation indicates that this change potentially undermines the ability to maintain confidentiality in legal proceedings, which goes against the spirit of protecting trade secrets under Rule 26(c). The change from 'shall' to 'should consider' coupled with the subjective 'reasonably advisable' significantly weakens the protection of confidential information, creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information that is in writing or other tangible form (including electronic form) shall be subject to this Agreement only if it is clearly marked as "Confidential" when disclosed by the Disclosing Party to a Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information that is in writing or other tangible form (including electronic form) should ideally be marked as "Confidential" when disclosed by the Disclosing Party to a Party, when feasible.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be' to 'should ideally be' and adding 'when feasible' introduces significant ambiguity and weakens the requirement for marking confidential information. This creates uncertainty regarding whether unmarked documents are covered.
perturbation.law_explanation: DUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. While not explicitly requiring labeling, clear marking as confidential is a common and strong indication of reasonable efforts. The modified text allows a party to claim that marking was not feasible, even when it could have been done, potentially weakening the trade secret protection and giving them plausible deniability.
perturbation.location: B. Confidential Information - General Non-Disclosure Provisions, 9. Identification of Confidential Information .

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the requirement for marking confidential information, creating ambiguity. The original text states that Confidential Information 'shall be subject to this Agreement only if it is clearly marked as "Confidential".' The changed text replaces 'shall be' with 'should ideally be' and adds 'when feasible,' introducing uncertainty. The law snippets, referencing Delaware Code Title 6, Chapter 20, address trade secrets and misappropriation, implying the importance of maintaining secrecy. Although the snippets don't explicitly mandate marking documents, the Law Explanation correctly notes that clear marking is a common practice, and the modified text allows a party to claim that marking was not feasible, even when it could have been done. DUTSA requires reasonable efforts, and the modification potentially weakens trade secret protection. The modification introduces ambiguity regarding whether unmarked documents are covered, which contradicts the implicit duty to maintain secrecy as described in the snippets.
